coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared carbon dots-based fluorescence turn on aptasensor for determination of carcinoembryonic antigen in pleural effusion
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Kang
Wang, Lanfang
Wen, Yingting
Wang, Tao
Teng, Yuanjie
Shen, Zhenlu
Pan, Zaifa
coredata.dc:description: Early detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is of great significance for the screening, diagnosis, monitoring and prognosis analysis of lung cancer. Herein, a novel fluorescence aptasensor with high signal-noise ratio (SNR) was constructed to achieve highly-sensitive detection of CEA relied upon the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs) and gold nanorods (AuNRs). Initially, AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer and NIR-CDs-DNA probe were prepared via the covalent bonding reaction between their corresponding carboxyl and amino groups, respectively. After DNA hybridization, the aptasensor was formed, meanwhile, the fluorescence of NIR-CDs was quenched by AuNRs@SiO2. Once CEA encountered the aptasensor, it would selectively combine with CEA aptamer to unwind the preformed DNA double-strand architecture thereby resulting in the NIR-CDs-DNA detach from the surface of AuNRs@SiO2. The attendant fluorescence recovery of NIR-CDs was linearly correlated with the concentration of CEA. According to this relationship, the NIR-CDs based “turn on” sensing system was constructed and exhibited prominent responses toward CEA in the concentration range of 0.1–5000 pg/mL and a relatively low detection limit (0.02 pg/mL). Moreover, it displayed high specificity against other biomarkers or proteins, good reproducibility and acceptable accuracy regarding human pleural effusion samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carcinoembryonic antigen
Near-infrared carbon dots
Gold nanorods
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of AuNRs@SiO2 and AuNRs@SiO2 NH2
Characterization of NIR-CDs and functionalized AuNRs
Investigation of the interaction between NIR-CDs and functionalized AuNRs
Analytical performance of the NIR-CDs-based fluorescence sensing strategy toward CEA
Selectivity and stability of NIR-CDs based aptasensor
Standard addition recovery experiments of the CEA in human pleural effusion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of NIR-CDs (A, B and C), AuNRs@SiO2 (D, E and F) and NIR-CDs-DNA-AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer probe (G, H and I).

(A) Fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of NIR-CDs (Insets: the photographs of NIR-CDs under (a) natural light and (b) 365 nm of UV light). Fluorescence emission spectra of the NIR-CDs tested under the condition of different (B) dilution factors and (C) excitation wavelengths. (D) FTIR spectra and (E) fluorescence emission spectra of NIR-CDs and NIR-CDs-DNA. (F) UV–Vis absorption spectra of AuNRs, AuNRs@SiO2 and AuNRs@SiO2 NH2. (G) ξ potentials and (H) FTIR spectra of AuNRs@SiO2, AuNRs@SiO2 NH2 and AuNRs@SiO2-Apatmer.

(A) The spectral overlap between emission spectrum of NIR-CDs and absorption spectrum of AuNRs. (B) Fluorescence mission spectra of NIR-CDs, NIR-CDs + AuNRs and NIR-CDs + AuNRs@SiO2. (C) Fluorescence influence of NIR-CDs by water, ethanol and CTAB. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of NIR-CDs, NIR-CDs-DNA + AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer

(A) Fluorescence mission spectra and (B) corresponding calibration curve of the fluorescent detection toward CEA using the as-preparaed aptasensor with a concentration 0.1–5000 pg/mL. (C) Selectivity and (D) stability of the proposed aptasensor.
NIR-CDs-based fluorescence turn-on aptasensor for detection of target CEA.
Standard addition recovery experiments of the CEA.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *1, 2, 3 represent three different negative human pleural effusion.

head.ce:title: Near-infrared carbon dots-based fluorescence turn on aptasensor for determination of carcinoembryonic antigen in pleural effusion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, 310014, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Early detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is of great significance for the screening, diagnosis, monitoring and prognosis analysis of lung cancer. Herein, a novel fluorescence aptasensor with high signal-noise ratio (SNR) was constructed to achieve highly-sensitive detection of CEA relied upon the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs) and gold nanorods (AuNRs). Initially, AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer and NIR-CDs-DNA probe were prepared via the covalent bonding reaction between their corresponding carboxyl and amino groups, respectively. After DNA hybridization, the aptasensor was formed, meanwhile, the fluorescence of NIR-CDs was quenched by AuNRs@SiO2. Once CEA encountered the aptasensor, it would selectively combine with CEA aptamer to unwind the preformed DNA double-strand architecture thereby resulting in the NIR-CDs-DNA detach from the surface of AuNRs@SiO2. The attendant fluorescence recovery of NIR-CDs was linearly correlated with the concentration of CEA. According to this relationship, the NIR-CDs based “turn on” sensing system was constructed and exhibited prominent responses toward CEA in the concentration range of 0.1–5000 pg/mL and a relatively low detection limit (0.02 pg/mL). Moreover, it displayed high specificity against other biomarkers or proteins, good reproducibility and acceptable accuracy regarding human pleural effusion samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), is a common cell surface glycoprotein as antigen in tissues and fetal cells, which involves in cell adhesion and plays a regulatory role in cell recognition and interaction [1]. Normally, CEA exists in blood with a tiny amount (<2.5 ng/mL), while it will be overexpressed and secreted into body fluids once tumors appearing. Therefore, determining the abnormal expression content of CEA in body fluids is of great significance for early warning, screening, diagnosis and prognosis analysis of cancer. Over the past few decades, several efficient analytical techniques mainly including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [2], immunochemiluminescence (CLIA) [3], electrochemical immunosensor [4–6], fluorescent spectrometry [7,8] and Raman spectroscopy [9] have been deployed for detection of CEA. Among them, fluorimetry is especially favored by researchers owning to the advantages of high sensitivity, wide linear range and reproducibility. Nevertheless, the high biotoxicity of most reported fluorescent probes (organic dyes, heavy metal chelates, quantum dots etc.) greatly limits their biological and clinical medicine applications.

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of nontoxic zero-dimensional carbon particles with sizes less than 10 nm, allows for superior optical property, water dispersibility and excellent biocompatibility as well as facile preparation and modification [10,11], which have provided an attractive application potential in the fields of biosensors [12–14], bioimaging [15,16], theranostic [17,18], opto-electronic devices [19,20] etc. Up to now, considerable research efforts have been devoted to synthetize CDs by various carbon sources ranging from laboratory-produced chemicals to natural products [21,22]. However, most of CDs only show intense blue-to green-light emission under excitation of ultraviolet (UV)- or blue-light, which is bound to be interfered by inherent blue auto-fluorescence of biological matrix in biological application [23]. In view of Kobayashi's reports on the extinction coefficient of oxidized hemoglobin, deoxyhemoglobin and water in serum, it can be clearly known that the extinction coefficient of these components is very large in the wavelength range of 400–600 nm, while the value of the extinction coefficient in the range of 650–900 nm is small [24]. Thus, the employment of near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs) to prepare fluorescent bioprobes can not only effectively avoid auto-fluorescence interference, but also own a strong penetrability to tissues [25–27]. Unfortunately, there are only few reports on the preparation of NIR-CDs. Ge and co-workers synthetized the several polythiophene derivatives-based CDs to achieve the wavelength tune from blue to NIR range under a changeless excitation [28]. Similarly, Hu et al. designed a wavelength conversion strategy to shift the wavelength of CDs from 400 to 710 nm by controlling the dehydration reaction during synthesis [29]. In particular, Lin's group used the microwave mediated heating strategy to prepare a highly water-dispersible NIR CDs [30]. Despite the three kinds of NIR-CDs possess expected optical properties, the cumbersome preparation processes and necessary demands on professional instruments limit their practical uses in a general case. Recently, Yang's group and Xiong's group employed a simple and universal solvothermal route to prepare NIR-CDs using small molecular carbon sources, successively [31,32]. By far, a little progress has been made in the synthesis strategy and primary bioimaging application of NIR-CDs, yet, in fact it should be noted that there has scarcely any work focusing on their biosensing applications. It is worthwhile mentioning that NIR-CDs are certain to display a large signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in biosensing because of the low extinction coefficient of biological matrix in NIR range, which is propitious to improve the sensitivity and accuracy of test results. There is thereby an urgent need to develop NIR-CDs based biosensors, but it is also a significant challenge to construct NIR-CDs labeled bio-probes with expected functions and design appropriate biosensing modes.

Herein, for the first attempt, NIR-CDs were utilized to construct a novel fluorescence “turn on” aptasensor for highly sensitive and accurate quantitative analysis of CEA based on the FRET between NIR-CDs and gold nanorods (AuNRs). As illustrated in Scheme 1
, the highly water-dispersible NIR-CDs with intense NIR emission (λ max = 680 nm) and a narrow emission band (FWHM ≈ 30 nm) were prepared by one-step solvothermal method using glutathione as a carbon source. In order to gain highly efficient FRET between donor (NIR-CDs) and acceptor (AuNRs), the absorption bands of AuNRs were tuned through changing their aspect ratios for actualizing maximum overlapping with the emission spectra of NIR-CDs [33]. Moreover, AuNRs were coated by SiO2 shell to reduce the effect of cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) on NIR-CDs and improve the coupling efficiency between AuNRs and DNA. With the help of aptamer and DNA hybridization, the fluorescence aptasensor was formed and successfully applied to the detection of CEA. Such an NIR-CDs-based fluorescence “turn on” aptasensor with a large SNR held an excellent biocompatibility and presented an excellent performance toward human pleural effusion samples.
The used agents and instruments are described in Supporting Information.

Glutathione (0.3402 g) was dissolved 10 mL of formamide and transferred to an autoclave for reacting 1 h in a 160 °C of oven. Afterwards, the reaction product was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 20 min in a centrifuge. The collected supernatant was diluted 5 times with water and dialyzed against a 3500 Da molecular weight dialysis bag for one week. Then, the dark green dispersion was filtered through a micropore filter (0.22 μm) to remove large particles. Finally, the obtained NIR-CDs were freeze-dried for further use.

Seed solution: 7.5 mL hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, 0.1 mol/L) and 100 μL of HAuCl4 (25 mmol/L) were diluted to 9.4 mL with water. After thoroughly mixing, 0.6 mL of a newly prepared NaBH4 solution (0.01 mol/L) was quickly added reacting for 2 min with vigorous stirring, during which the solution turned from bright yellow to brown. The reaction solution was allowed to aging for 2 h in a 30 °C of incubator. Growth solution: 0.5 mL of HAuCl4 (25 mmol/L), 25 μL of AgNO3 (0.1 mol/L) and 250 μL of H2SO4 (1 mol/L) were successively added to 25 mL of CTAB solution (0.1 mol/L) with stirring. Then, 200 μL of ascorbic acid (0.1 mol/L) was slowly added into the above mixture. Followed by, 60 μL of gold seed dispersion was quickly added into the growth solution under vigorous agitation. Finally, the mixture was allowed to stand at 37 °C for 12 h to form gold nanorods (AuNRs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AuNRs@SiO2 and AuNRs@SiO2 NH2
ce:section.ce:para: AuNRs@SiO2: The newly prepared 30 mL of AuNRs dispersion was initially centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 20 min to obtain pure AuNRs precipitate. To coating silica on its surface, the precipitate was redispersed into 20 mL of a CTAB solution at a concentration of 3 mmol/L. Subsequently, 200 μL of sodium hydroxide solution (100 mmol/L) was added to the dispersion under stirring. 30 μL of TEOS (20% in methanol) was added thereto every 30 min for a total of three times and stirred at room temperature for 2 days. AuNRs@SiO2 NH2: 1 mL of AuNRs@SiO2 was mixed with 1 mL of ethanol and 5‰ of 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTS) under vigorous agitation. After mixing evenly, the mixture was reacted under mild stirring for 12 h at room temperature. The obtained reaction product was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min in a centrifuge, washed twice with ethanol, and then dispersed into 1 mL of ethanol.

First, to activate the carboxyl group of aptamers, 12 μL of aptamers (100 μmol/L), 100 μL of EDC solution (20 mg/mL) and 100 μL of NHS solution (20 mg/mL) were mixed for reacting 30 min at room temperature under vigorous agitation. Then, the activated aptamers were reacted with 1 mL of the AuNRs@SiO2 NH2 dispersion for 12 h under mild stirring conditions. Afterwards, the reaction product was transferred to a 2 mL of centrifuge tube to centrifuge at 12000 rpm for 10 min, and the unreacted supernatant was discarded. The precipitate was washed with water twice and redispersed in 1 mL of PBS buffer solution to obtain the AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer. The NIR-CDs-DNA was prepared according to the similar approach. In brief, the carboxyl group of NIR-CDs (1 mL, 0.5 mg/mL) was first activated by the EDC (10 mg/mL) and NHS (10 mg/mL) mixed solution. Subsequently, to ensure the complete reaction of complementary DNA, a certain dosage of amino-modified complementary DNA (100 μmol/L) was reacted with 1 mL of activated NIR-CDs for 12 h with mild stirring. The reaction product was centrifuged to collect the supernatant for obtaining the NIR-CDs-DNA.

2 mL of each AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer was mixed with the CDs-DNA PBS dispersion, and the intermixture was placed in a 37 °C of incubator for 60 min to cause the DNA hybridization, followed by centrifugation and washing to obtain a fluorescent aptasensor.

First, different concentration gradients of CEA standard solutions ranging from 2 × 10−12 to 2 × 10−8 g/mL were prepared. 200 μL of different concentrations of CEA were mixed with 200 μL of the aptasensor dispersion and incubated for 60 min at 37 °C. Blank control: 200 μL of PBS buffer solution was added into 200 μL of aptasensor dispersion under the same conditions. The fluorescence quenching and recovery were measured after the completion of reaction.

The blank pleural effusion sample of healthy people was provided by Zhejiang People's Hospital. 40 μL of pleural effusion blank sample was first diluted 100-fold with PBS buffer solution, and 450 μL of each was taken out and mixed with different concentrations of CEA solution (2 × 10−10, 1 × 10−9 and 2 × 10−9 g/mL). The fluorescence recovery value was measured by mixing 200 μL of the above mixture with 200 μL of the fluorescent aptasensor dispersion after the incubation of them for 60 min at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NIR-CDs and functionalized AuNRs
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 describes that NIR-CDs were synthetized via a simple solvothermal reaction between glutathione carbon precursor and formamide solvent. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was initially used to observe the microstructures of NIR-CDs. It can be seen from Fig. 1 A and B, the NIR-CDs are quasi-spherical with a mean size of ∼3.6 nm and have no large particles or aggregation, indicating the reaction process is steady, and no adverse side reaction occurred. Fig. 1C shows clear lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which is in accordance with the (100) facets of graphite, indicating a graphite-like structure contained in this material. As shown in Fig. S1, the sizes distribution of NIR-CDs is very narrow and their average hydrated particle size is ∼6.9 nm, which is slightly larger the size obtained by TEM mainly due to the action of water molecules on the surface of the NIR-CDs.

The AuNRs exhibit a kind of capsule-shaped structures with the length of ∼40 nm and the width of ∼20 nm (Fig. 1D). In order to make the surface of the AuNRs easy to modify and not affected by the external environment, this experiment chose to treat the AuNRs with silica. Furthermore, to reduce the distance between energy donor (NIR-CDs) and receptor (AuNRs), the thickness of the cladding layer was adjusted in this experiment. Fig. 1E shows that the AuNRs has been successfully wrapped with a SiO2 coating with a thickness of about 5–10 nm after the silicon-coated treatment. The lattice fringes of the AuNRs can be clearly seen from Fig. 1F, and the interplanar spacing is calculated to be 0.238 nm. The TEM images of NIR-CDs-DNA-AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer probe were shown in Fig. 1G, H and 1I. It can be observed clearly from Fig. 1G and H, some ultra-small dot-like particles are scattered around the silica shell. It can be concluded that the particles attached to the outer surface of silica shell are NIR-CDs by the HRTEM images of Fig. 1C, F and 1I, which directly confirmed that the aptasensor was structured successfully.

Fig. 2A shows that the optimal fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength of the as-prepared NIR-CDs is about 420 and 680 nm, respectively. In addition, the NIR-CDs exhibits a high quantum yield (16.4%) under the optimal excitation. From the insets, we can clear observe that NIR-CDs solution shows a green color under nature light, while it appears dull red fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The phenomenon can prove that the experiment has prepared CDs with emission in the NIR region. Fig. S2 shows that the as-prepared NIR-CDs have two UV–vis characteristic absorption bands located at 350–450 and 550–750 nm, respectively. The first absorption bands mainly caused by the π→π* transition of aromatic C=C bond. And the second band is ascribed to the aromatic π→π* and n→π* transitions regarding C=O, C=S and C=N bonds. The fluorescence excitation band of NIR-CDs was overlapped with its major UV–vis absorption band, indicating that their NIR emissions are closely interrelated with these absorption bands caused by the relevant structures [27]. In most cases, luminous intensities of fluorophor are closely related to their concentrations. As shown in Fig. 2 B, the fluorescence intensities of the NIR-CDs increase first and then decrease with the dilution multiple increases, and the optimal dilution factor is three times. In addition, the fluorescence excitation correlation of NIR-CDs was also examined. As the excitation wavelength is gradually changed from 380 nm to 470 nm, NIR-CDs emerge an excitation-independent emission feature and have the optimal excitation wavelength of ∼420 nm and emission wavelength of 683 nm (Fig. 2C). In Fig. 2E, when the NIR-CDs is coupled with the complementary DNA, the emission peak position of NIR-CDs shifts from the original 683 nm–648 nm, may be caused by the chemical bonding between carboxyl group of the NIR-CDs and amino-group of the complementary DNA. In Fig. 2D, a wide FTIR absorption band emerges at 3000–3500 cm−1 and two characteristic absorption peaks at 1087 and 1159 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibration of OH and NH bonds, CO bonds and CN bonds, respectively. The amide bond is determined by the peaks at 1645 and 1400 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of C=O and CN of the amide, respectively. NIR-CDs-DNA has an absorption peak at 3130 cm−1 representing the stretching vibration of NH2, and absorption at 1693 cm−1 represents the amide I band.[35] Two characteristic absorption peaks at 1560 and 1309 cm−1 represent the amide II and the amide III band. This phenomenon indicates that the NIR-CDs have been successfully linked to the complementary DNA.

As we known, the outstanding optical properties of AuNRs are ascribed to surface plasmon resonance. The free electrons existing inside the metal nanoparticles have a fixed frequency oscillation. When the incident light frequency is consistent with the free electron vibration frequency, resonance coupling occurs, so that free electrons generate coherent oscillation on the surface of the nanoparticle. The electronic oscillation is the SPR effect. This property makes the AuNRs show a strong absorption peak on the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum. The AuNRs are capsule-shaped nanoparticles with a transverse plasma absorption peak (TSPR) and a longitudinal plasma absorption peak (LSPR) corresponding to two characteristic dimensions of the horizontal axis and the vertical axis, respectively. Therefore, in this experiment, the ultraviolet visible spectrum was mainly used to study the optical properties of the prepared AuNRs. As shown in Fig. 2F, AuNRs, silica-coated gold nanorods (AuNRs@SiO2), and aminated gold nanorods (AuNRs@SiO2 NH2) all exhibit an absorption peak at about 520 nm, which represents the transverse absorption peak of AuNRs. The longitudinal absorption peaks of AuNRs are mainly around 700 nm, while the vertical axis of AuNRs@SiO2 and AuNRs@SiO2 NH2 is absorbed at about 680 nm. After the silicon-coated treatment and amination, the blue shift of absorption peaks may be caused by the dispersing of the AuNRs@SiO2 and AuNRs@SiO2 NH2 in ethanol and the AuNRs in the aqueous solution. Because AuNRs are easy to be precipitated by ethanol, and the 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTS) used in the amination of AuNRs is easily hydrolyzed, it is necessary to disperse AuNRs@SiO2 and AuNRs@SiO2 NH2 in absolute ethanol and disperse AuNRs in water. In addition, the surface functionalization of AuNRs can be reflected by the change of ζ potential (Fig. 2G). The ζ potential of AuNRs was previously measured to be +22.7 mV, and it would change to be −15.5 mV after the silicon-coated treatment. The ζ potential of the AuNRs@SiO2 NH2 was measured to be +12.4 mV and it will change to be −4.23 mV after coupling with the aptamer, because the positively charged amino groups of the AuNRs@SiO2 NH2 are reacted with the carboxyl group on the aptamer DNA. It can be known that AuNRs@SiO2 NH2 has been successfully ligated to the aptamer. For the FTIR spectra of AuNRs@SiO2, AuNRs@SiO2 NH2 and AuNRs@SiO2-Apatmer (Fig. 2H), the stretching vibration of SiOSi present at 1087 cm−1 by the AuNRs@SiO2 indicates the presence of SiOH bonds, which proves that silicon-coated modification is achieved. The in-plane deformation vibration of NH located at 1640 cm−1, and the peak at 985 cm−1, 798 cm−1 and 557 cm−1 indicates the presence of SiOSiNH2 bond, which proves that the amination for AuNRs@SiO2 has been achieved. AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer has an absorption peak at 3130 cm−1 representing the stretching vibration of NH2, and absorption at 1639 cm−1 represents the amide I band. Two clear absorption bands at 1533 cm−1 (amide II band) and 1400 cm−1 (amide III band) are consistent with that of NIR-CDs-DNA, indicating that the AuNRs@SiO2 has been successfully coupled to the aptamer [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the interaction between NIR-CDs and functionalized AuNRs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3A shows that there is optimal spectral overlap between the energy donor (NIR-CDs) and receptor (AuNRs) demonstrating the maximum FRET efficiency. It is known to all that the FRET is related to the distance between the donor and acceptor. Thus, the effect of distance change on FRET efficiency should be investigated. In general, the suitable distance for FRET is between 1 and 10 nm, and the FRET efficiency is inversely proportional to the distance. Theoretically, AuNRs ought to have a stronger quenching to NIR-CDs than AuNRs@SiO2. Abnormally, AuNRs@SiO2 nanoparticles exhibit a stronger quenching effect than AuNRs (Fig. 3 B). From the photographs (a), (b) and (c), it can also come to the same conclusion. In order to identify possible causes of the abnormal phenomenon, we investigated the influence of CTAB and solvent on NIR-CDs. It is proved by experiments that CTAB has obvious-enhancement effect on the fluorescence of NIR-CDs, leading to the lower quenching efficiency on NIR-CDs of CTAB-stabilized AuNRs. Furthermore, we also found that CTAB and ethanol can result in the clear blue shift of NIR-CDs's fluorescence emission peaks (Fig. 3C). In addition, CTAB has certain biological toxicity. Therefore, AuNRs@SiO2 with the higher quenching rate than AuNRs toward the NIR-CDs and good biocompatibility were chosen to construct fluorescence aptasensor. As it can be seen from Fig. 3D, after mixing NIR-CDs-DNA with AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer, the fluorescence intensity of NIR-CDs-DNA were reduced by about 70%, indicates that the aptasensor was structured successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the NIR-CDs-based fluorescence sensing strategy toward CEA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S4 presents the test condition optimization concerning the incubation time and pH. The fluorescence intensity of NIR-CDs decreases with time before, and then tends to be stable until 60 min. Thus, the reaction time of the quenching system is set to 60 min. Similarly, the optimal fluorescence recovery time is also set to 60 min (Fig. S5). It can be seen from Fig. S6, the highest fluorescence recovery value can be obtained under the condition of pH = 7. However, in consideration of the physiological pH, the value of 7.4 was selected as the optimal pH value. With the help of DNA hybridization between as-prepared AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer and NIR-CDs-DNA, a novel fluorescent “turn on” aptasensor with NIR luminescence signals was constructed successfully and presented the outstanding sensing performance toward target CEA. Fig. 4 A reveals that the fluorescence restoration degree of NIR-CDs based aptasensor is positively correlated with the CEA concentration, which is the important basis for quantitative detection of the target. Clearly, the fluorescence readout of the aptasensor increases with the increase of CEA concentration and exhibit an established linear correlation toward CEA concentration range (0.1–5000 pg/mL) (Fig. 4B). Their linear relevant regression equation is obtained from the fitting curve, as follows: I = 3704150 + 841400 × CCEA. The high linear correlation degree (R2 = 0.98703) and low relative standard deviation (RSD, 6.3%) implying high precision of the NIR-CDs based sensing platform (Fig. 4B, inset). In particular, according to the principle of triple signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3), the aptasensor holds a lower limit of detection (LOD, 0.02 pg/mL) than most other reported methods (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of NIR-CDs based aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Although the aptasensor showed excellent performance toward CEA, its selectivity against other biomarkers or proteins and long-term stability needed to be investigated. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) and prostate specific antigen (PSA) as two relevant cancer markers, a representative enzyme (Trypsin) and the frequently-used albumen (bovine serum albumin, BSA) were employed to checkout the selectivity of NIR-CDs based aptasensor. As shown in Fig. 4C, different from the substantial fluorescence recovery of aptasensor caused by CEA, the detected fluorescence output that regarding AFP, PSA, Trypsin, and BSA are roughly the same as the background signals (NIR-CDs-DNA-AuNRs@SiO2-Aptamer probe), indicating these non-specific targets basically could not trigger the fluorescence recovery. The results clearly authenticated the as-prepared aptasensor possessed a satisfactory selectivity for CEA. After more than 20 days of storage at 4 °C in darkness, the signal output of NIR-CDs based aptasensor can still maintain ∼94% of the original fluorescent intensity demonstrating the acceptable stability (Fig. 4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard addition recovery experiments of the CEA in human pleural effusion
ce:section.ce:para: Three negative human pleural effusion samples obtained from Zhejiang People's Hospital were utilized to actualize standard addition recovery experiments for verifying the detection accuracy of the method towards the simulated clinical samples. The results showed that CEA was not detected in the three human three pleural effusion negative samples under the experimental conditions. As shown in Table 1 , the aptasensor manifested an acceptable analytical accuracy depending on the good addition recovery of CEA in human pleural effusion samples (90.6–107.0%) and a low RSD (≤3.7%), which also pointed out the potential application of this technique to clinical samples.

In summary, a novel fluorescence sensing platform has been developed for highly-sensitive detection of CEA based on the FRET between NIR-CDs and AuNRs. The as-prepared NIR-CDs with excellent water dispersibility, ease of modification, relatively high luminescent efficiency and photostability had proven to be a promising candidate for fabricating biosensors. The effects of spectral overlap and distance on FRET between NIR-CDs and AuNRs have been investigated and applied to design NIR-CDs-based aptasensor. The fabricated aptasensor can effectively avoid inherent auto-fluorescence of biological matrix leading to the higher accuracy and sensitivity toward complex target CEA samples than most conventional detection methods of CEA. What really matters is that the current sensing mode provides a guidance to assess the construction of other NIR biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21804119), Key projects of Zhejiang Natural Science Foundation (Project No. LZ18B050002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2019.03.056.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Micro-plate magnetic chemiluminescence immunoassay and its applications in carcinoembryonic antigen analysis
Sensitive sandwich ELISA based on a gold nanoparticle layer for cancer detection
A rapid and highly sensitive portable chemiluminescent immunosensor of carcinoembryonic antigen based on immunomagnetic separation in human serum
Sandwich-type electrochemical immunosensor for CEA detection based on Ag/MoS2@Fe3O4 and an analogous ELISA method with total internal reflection microscopy
Enhanced peroxidase-like properties of Au@Pt DNs/NG/Cu2+ and application of sandwich-type electrochemical immunosensor for highly sensitive detection of CEA
Surface-enhanced electrochemiluminescence of Ru@SiO2 for ultrasensitive detection of carcinoembryonic antigen
A fluorescent biosensor based on molybdenum disulfide nanosheets and protein aptamer for sensitive detection of carcinoembryonic antigen
Three kinds of DNA-directed nanoclusters cooperating with graphene oxide for assaying mucin 1, carcinoembryonic antigen and cancer antigen 125
Combination assay of lung cancer associated serum markers using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
